steps.title: Identify parallel line conditions
steps.content:
A system has at least one solution unless the equations represent distinct parallel lines, which occurs when one equation is a scalar multiple of the other.

Test equation I: $3x + 13.5y = 10.5$. Multiply the original $2x + 9y = 7$ by \$1.5$:

$$1.5(2x + 9y) = 1.5(7) \quad \Rightarrow \quad 3x + 13.5y = 10.5$$

This matches equation I exactly. Both equations represent the same line with infinitely many solutions, so equation I works.

steps.title: Test equation II and conclude
steps.content:
For equation II: $3x - 13.5y = 10.5$, check if it's a scalar multiple of $2x + 9y = 7$. This requires some scalar $k$ such that:

$$2k = 3 \quad \text{and} \quad 9k = -13.5$$

From the first: $k = 1.5$. From the second: $k = -1.5$. These contradict, so equation II is not a scalar multiple. The lines are not parallel and intersect at one point. Equation II works.

Both I and II yield systems with at least one solution. That matches choice C — C is correct.
